title: The practice of being patient with yourself when progress feels invisible
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You're doing the work. Why doesn't it feel like it? 

 I sat on my couch last Tuesday and stared at the wall for forty minutes. Not meditating. Not dissoc...
htmlContent:
You're doing the work. Why doesn't it feel like it?

I sat on my couch last Tuesday and stared at the wall for forty minutes. Not meditating. Not dissociating. Just staring. Because I was tired. Bone tired. The kind of tired that comes from years of showing up to your own healing and feeling like you're running on a treadmill. You're moving. You're sweating. But when you look around, the scenery hasn't changed. Right?

I get it. I really do. There's this unspoken rule in recovery culture that says if you just do the right things, you'll feel better. Do your morning pages. Go to therapy. Breathe. Set boundaries. And you'll wake up one day and everything will be different. But here's the thing: that's not how complex trauma recovery works. At all.

Peter Levine, the guy who literally wrote the book on somatic experiencing, talks about pendulation. The idea that healing isn't linear. It's a swing. You move toward the pain, then you move away from it. Toward. Away. Toward. Away. And most of the time, that away part feels like you're losing ground. But you're not. You're just letting your nervous system catch its breath.

I've been in recovery for seven years. Seven. And some days I still feel like I'm in year one. That's not an exaggeration. That's the reality of working with a nervous system that spent decades learning to survive. You can't undo that in a year. Or two. Or five. But our culture wants quick fixes. It wants before and after photos. It wants testimonies that say "I did this one thing and everything changed." And that pressure? It makes us feel like failures when our progress stays invisible.

So let's talk about what invisible progress actually looks like. And why being patient with yourself isn't just some wellness platitude. It's survival.

The nervous system doesn't care about your timeline

Stephen Porges, the researcher behind polyvagal theory, spent decades mapping out how our autonomic nervous system actually works. And one of the things he found is that your nervous system is always scanning for safety. Always. Even when you're asleep. Even when you think you're fine. It's running in the background, checking the environment, checking your internal state, checking the people around you. And it makes decisions about whether you're safe or not in milliseconds. Way before your conscious brain even knows what's happening.

So when you're in recovery, you're basically asking your nervous system to unlearn decades of survival patterns. Patterns that kept you alive. Patterns that your body trusts, even if they hurt. And your body is not interested in your timeline. It doesn't care that you have a therapy appointment next Tuesday. It doesn't care that you read a book about attachment theory. It cares about one thing: staying alive.

I remember a period where I was doing everything right. I was in therapy. I was doing somatic exercises. I was reading Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana, trying to understand my own nervous system map. And I still had a panic attack in the grocery store because someone touched my shoulder from behind. I felt like a fraud. Like all that work meant nothing. But here's what I didn't understand then: that panic attack wasn't proof that I was failing. It was proof that my nervous system was still doing its job. It was protecting me from a perceived threat. And the fact that I recovered from that panic attack in twenty minutes instead of three hours? That was progress. But I couldn't see it because I was looking for the big, dramatic change.

Wild, right? The most important progress often happens in the spaces we don't think to look.

What invisible progress actually looks like

Let me give you some examples of progress that looks like nothing. Because I think we need a new map. A map that doesn't just mark the mountaintops but also marks the flat ground where you learned to stand still without falling.

Invisible progress is the moment you notice you're dissociating and you come back to your body in five minutes instead of two hours. It's the day you feel the urge to numb out with food or alcohol or scrolling, and you pause for just one breath before you do it. Even if you still do it. That pause matters. That pause is your prefrontal cortex saying "hey, I see you" to your limbic system. That's neural integration happening. That's Dan Siegel's whole thing about the brain learning to coordinate different parts of itself.

Invisible progress is the argument you had with your partner where you raised your voice but didn't say the thing you usually say. The thing that cuts deep. The thing you regret for days. You still got loud. You still got triggered. But you didn't go there. That's not failure. That's a boundary with yourself. And that is huge.

Invisible progress is the morning you wake up and feel the familiar heaviness in your chest, and instead of fighting it or spiraling into "why am I still depressed," you just say "okay, I see you" and you make your coffee anyway. That's not toxic positivity. That's acceptance. That's you learning to coexist with your pain instead of being consumed by it. And that skill? That's one of the most advanced recovery skills there is. But nobody gives you a gold star for it because it looks like you're just having a normal day.

I've started keeping a different kind of journal. Not a gratitude journal. Not a "what went well" journal. A journal where I write down the small, weird, almost invisible shifts I notice. Like "today I felt anger in my chest and didn't immediately shame myself for it." Or "I noticed I was holding my breath and I let it out without judging myself for holding it in the first place." These sound ridiculous when you say them out loud. But they're the actual building blocks of recovery. Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," talks about how trauma lives in the body. Which means healing also lives in the body. In the small sensations. The small releases. The small moments of presence that you can easily miss if you're only looking for big breakthroughs.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. And in it, she describes recovery as happening in stages. But here's the thing: those stages don't move in a straight line. You might be in stage three and then something happens and you're back in stage one for a week. That's not regression. That's your system doing a safety check. It's like your body is saying "let me make sure that ground is still solid before I take another step forward." And you have to let it do that. You have to be patient with the checking. Because if you rush, your body will just dig its heels in deeper.

The patience that isn't passive

I want to be really clear about something. Being patient with yourself is not the same as giving up. It's not the same as settling for less than you deserve. And it's definitely not the same as toxic positivity that tells you to just "trust the process" while you're drowning.

The patience I'm talking about is active. It's fierce. It's the decision to keep showing up even when you can't see the results. It's the willingness to do the boring, repetitive, unglamorous work of regulation. Of breathing. Of noticing. Of coming back to your body over and over and over again. And it's the radical act of not shaming yourself for needing to do it again.

I think about this in terms of gardening. You plant a seed. You water it. You give it sunlight. And for weeks, nothing happens above the ground. But underneath, the roots are growing. The seed is cracking open. The whole infrastructure of the plant is being built. And if you dug it up every day to check if it was working, you'd kill it. The same is true for your nervous system. It needs time underground. Time where nothing looks like it's happening. Time where you just keep showing up and doing the small things and trusting that the roots are growing.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. And one of the things she talks about is how boundaries aren't about controlling other people. They're about taking care of yourself. And that takes practice. It takes patience. You're not going to set a perfect boundary the first time. Or the tenth time. But each time you try, you're building a new neural pathway. You're teaching your nervous system that it's okay to say no. That you can survive someone being disappointed in you. That's invisible progress. But it's real.

Stay with me here, because this is the part that's hard to hear. You might be doing the work for years before you feel a significant shift. That's not because you're doing it wrong. That's because the damage was deep. And deep damage takes time to heal. Not because you're broken. But because your body is thorough. It wants to make sure it's really safe before it lets go of the old patterns. And that thoroughness? That's actually a sign of health. Your survival strategies kept you alive. Your body is not going to abandon them lightly. And it shouldn't. You have to earn its trust. And earning trust takes time.

How to practice patience when you're running on empty

So what do you actually do? How do you practice patience when your nervous system is screaming for relief and nothing seems to be working? I've got a few things that help me. Take what works, leave the rest.

First, lower the bar. I mean really lower it. If your goal for today is to brush your teeth and eat something, that's enough. If your goal is to notice one breath, that's enough. We've been sold this idea that healing requires huge efforts. But actually, healing requires consistency more than intensity. Five minutes of regulation every day is better than two hours once a month. Your nervous system learns through repetition, not through heroic effort. So make it small. Make it doable. Make it boring. That's how real change happens.

Second, stop comparing your insides to other people's outsides. This one is brutal, I know. But you have no idea what someone else's recovery actually looks like. You see their social media post about a breakthrough. You don't see the three weeks of crying in the bathroom that came before it. You see the "I finally set a boundary" post. You don't see the anxiety attack that followed. Everyone's invisible progress looks different. And comparing yours to someone else's is just a recipe for shame. And shame shuts down the nervous system. It makes healing harder. So try to catch yourself when you're comparing and just say "I don't actually know their full story. And I don't need to."

Third, find ways to mark the small wins. This is where a tool like a workbook can help. A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Arielle Schwartz. It gives you concrete things to track. Things that help you see the invisible progress. Because sometimes you need an external structure to help you notice what your own brain is filtering out. And that's okay. That's not cheating. That's using tools.

Fourth, talk to your inner critic. I know, I know. It sounds weird. But Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, talks about how the parts of us that criticize and shame are actually trying to protect us. They think that if they keep you small and perfect and always striving, you'll be safe. So when you notice that voice saying "you're not doing enough," try responding with curiosity instead of agreement. Say "I see you're worried. Thank you for trying to protect me. But I'm actually okay right now." It might feel fake at first. But over time, it shifts the relationship. And that shift is invisible progress.

And finally, let yourself grieve. Because part of patience is acknowledging that this is hard. That it's not fair. That you didn't ask for this. That you wish you were further along. That you're tired. Grieve it. Let yourself feel the disappointment and the anger and the sadness. Because when you suppress those feelings, they just get louder. When you let them move through you, they eventually settle. And then you can get back to the work with a little more space inside.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical
